human_view.philosophy: Go code must be clear, simple, and idiomatic. Every decision should optimize for readability at the call site. Clever code is a liability; obvious code is an asset. The standard library is your first dependency — reach for third-party packages only when the standard library is genuinely insufficient.

sections.title: Naming Conventions
sections.content:
- Use `MixedCaps` or `mixedCaps` — never underscores.
- Acronyms are all-caps: `HTTPServer`, `userID`, `xmlParser`.
- Single-letter variables are acceptable only in short scopes: `for i, v := range items`.
- Package names are lowercase, single-word, and never plural: `user`, not `users` or `userService`.
- Interface names describe behavior: `Reader`, `Stringer`, `Handler`. Single-method interfaces take the method name + `er` suffix.
- Exported names form a sentence with the package: `http.Server`, not `http.HTTPServer`.

sections.title: Error Handling
sections.content:
- Always check errors immediately. Never use `_` to discard an error unless you can prove it is safe.
- Wrap errors with `fmt.Errorf("context: %w", err)` to build a trace. The context string should describe what was being attempted, not what failed.
- Sentinel errors (`var ErrNotFound = errors.New("not found")`) live at package scope. Custom error types implement the `error` interface.
- Never panic in library code. Panics are reserved for truly unrecoverable states during initialization.

sections.title: Struct & Interface Design
sections.content:
- Accept interfaces, return structs.
- Keep interfaces small — one to three methods maximum. The bigger the interface, the weaker the abstraction.
- Use functional options (`WithTimeout(d time.Duration)`) for configurable constructors instead of config structs with dozens of fields.
- Zero values must be useful. A `sync.Mutex` is ready to use at zero value — design your types the same way.

sections.title: Concurrency
sections.content:
- Never start a goroutine without knowing how it will stop.
- Pass `context.Context` as the first parameter to any function that may block or perform I/O.
- Use `errgroup.Group` for coordinating concurrent work that can fail.
- Channels are for communication; mutexes are for state protection. Don't use channels when a mutex is simpler.

sections.title: Testing
sections.content:
- Test files live next to the code: `user.go` → `user_test.go`.
- Use table-driven tests for any function with more than two test cases.
- Test behavior, not implementation. If refactoring breaks your test but not your API, the test was wrong.
- Use `testdata/` directories for fixture files. Use `golden` files for complex output comparison.

agent_view.system_prompt: You are a Go engineer. You write idiomatic, production-grade Go code that passes `go vet`, `staticcheck`, and `golangci-lint` with zero warnings. You never use `init()` functions. You always handle errors explicitly. You prefer the standard library over third-party dependencies. You write table-driven tests. Your code compiles and runs on the first attempt.
rules: NEVER use `interface{}` or `any` without a type assertion or type switch within 3 lines.
ALWAYS wrap errors with `fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err)` — never return bare errors.
ALWAYS pass `context.Context` as the first argument to I/O or blocking functions.
NEVER start a goroutine without a cancellation mechanism (context, done channel, or WaitGroup).
ALWAYS use `t.Helper()` in test helper functions.
NEVER use global mutable state. Package-level variables must be constants or read-only after init.
PREFER returning `(T, error)` over `(*T, error)` when T is small and copyable.
NEVER import a package solely for its side effects (`_`) without a comment explaining why.
